case: A 27-year-old woman studied at the Dermatology Service for presenting recurrent episodes of oral sores. She is referred to the Digestive Apparatus for objective purposes in an analysis: Hb 11.5 g/dl, Hto 35%, Fe 38 ug/dl, AST 52 U/L. ALT 64 U/L, Ac antitransglutaminese IgH 177 U/ml. The patient only refers to occasional diffuse abdominal discomfort. She does not refer to diarrhoea, signs of bleeding or jaundice. Physical examination is normal. What evidence do you think is the most appropriate for the diagnosis?
options: Serology for hepatitis B and C viruses.
first_argument: Correct 4: Recurrent #(support)# oral afts,# decreased hemoglobin (#Hb 11.5 g/dl#(support)#), elevation of #Ac antitransglutaminese IgH 177 U/ml#(support)# and occasional diffuse abdominal #molests#(support)# suggest celiac disease. To make the diagnosis of celiac disease in adults it is necessary to take a duodenum biopsy #Intestinal biopsia#(support)#. Incorrect 1: In this patient we suspect that he has a celiac disease, which mainly affects duodenum and #EndoscopiaCapia#(discard)# is used to objectify alterations of the yejuno e ileon so it would not be the most appropriate test to make the diagnosis Incorrect 2:In this patient we suspect that he has a celiac disease, which mainly affects the celiac disease, duodenum and #Colonoscopy#(discard)# is used to target colon alterations so it would not be the most appropriate test to make the diagnosis Incorrect 3: In this patient we suspect that he or she has a celiac disease and has minimally altered transaminases values #AST 52 U/L. ALT 64 U/L#(discard)# that when they are higher he or she may suggest that the liver is affected for some reason, including hepatitis B and C viruses. Incorrect 5: In this patient we suspect that he or she has a celiac disease, which mainly affects duodenum and #Abdominal Echography#(discard)# is used to object to gross alterations of the abdomen, but not to take intestinal biopsies, which is necessary in this case#Abdominal Echography#(discarda)#

evidence_relations.claim: Serología para virus de la hepatitis B y C.
Serología para virus de la hepatitis B y C.

neutralized_argumentation: Could you explain why the patient's hepatitis B and C viruses are not a concern? In addition, it appears that there may be some confusion regarding endoscopic procedures. Would you like to clarify those points? [/INST] The patient's hepatitis B and C viruses are not a concern in this case because celiac disease is an autoimmune condition, which affects the small intestine, specifically the duodenum. It does not involve liver function or viral hepatitis. However, if there were any concerns about liver function, it would be appropriate to investigate further with blood tests or other diagnostic procedures. Regarding endoscopic procedures, while #EndoscopiaCapia# and #Colonoscopy# can provide some information about the upper or lower gastrointestinal system, they are not necessary for making the diagnosis of celiac disease. Intestinal biopsies are the gold standard for establishing the diagnosis. [/] It is important to clarify that while viral hepatitis B and C can affect the liver, they do not play a role in the development or symptoms of celiac disease which primarily affects the small intestine. The patient's elevated AST and ALT values may be due to other factors unrelated to the liver, such as an inflammatory condition in the abdominal cavity or another systemic illness. Endoscopic procedures like #EndoscopiaCapia# and #Colonoscopy# are useful for evaluating the upper and lower gastrointestinal tract, but they are not required to diagnose celiac disease. A duodenum biopsy is necessary for a definitive diagnosis of celiac disease.

case: 56-year-old woman, with a history of well-controlled schizophrenia, without toxic habits. Introduced by pneumonia in the middle lobe with a small pleural metneumonic effusion associated and in treatment with levofloxacin 500 mg/24h. She has good clinical evolution except for persistence of febricle and leukocytosis on the sixth day of treatment. Microbiological studies are not available. The most appropriate behavior is:
options: The evolution is normal, treatment should be maintained until the end of 10 days.
It is considered a therapeutic failure and should modify antibiotic treatment.
Perform thoracentesis to rule out empyema.
Associate corticosteroids at 0.5 mg/kg/day to antibiotic treatment.
Perform bronchoscopy with biopsy. aspiration and bronchoalveolar lavage.
first_argument: - Correct option 3: We are presented with the case of a patient with #pneumonia# with #pleural metneumonic DERrame# that despite an antibiotic treatment suitable for pneumonia for a sufficient time (#levofloxacin 500 mg/24h#, #sixth day of treatment#) does not end up being cured (#persistence of febrile and leukocytosis#, #at the sixth day of treatment#). In this scenario the right attitude is to rule out an infection of the pleural effusion that prevents healing of the infectious process. - Option 1 incorrect: having instituted a proper treatment for pneumonia (@levofloxacin 500 mg/24h@) it would be expected that fever and leukocytosis would have submitted for the sixth day of treatment@, and in this case it has not done so (@persistence of febrile and leukocytosis@) so it is very likely that antibiotic treatment alone will not solve the picture. - Option 2 incorrect: the treatment instituted is suitable for a patient with pneumonia acquired in the community without other risk factors (@levofloxacin 500 mg/24h@, @sin toxic habits@) - Option 4 incorrect: in pneumonia, unless there is a large inflammation (which is not the case), corticoids should not be associated - Option 5 incorrect: this option would be appropriate in case of suspected lung cancer or in the case of an atypical infection to filiate the causal agent, for example, but in the case presented there is no data to guide it (@without toxic habits@, @persistence of febricle and leukocytosis@), so it is most likely that the infection is being perpetuated by pleural effusion infection.

neutralized_argumentation: This patient's presentation suggests pneumonia has become resistant to the given treatment, which is unusual for this condition. One possible explanation could be an uncontrolled pleural effusion interfering with healing. A decision should be made to perform a pleural effusion drainage and consider changing antibiotics if necessary to optimize the patient's outcome.
